title: Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns

learningObjectives.text: Explain components as building blocks using a new example and justify the result.
Apply jsx syntax and purpose using a new example and justify the result.
Explain react's separation-of-concerns model using a new example and justify the result.

whyThisMatters: The ideas in components, jsx, and separation of concerns recur throughout later React work, reducing memorization and debugging later. It belongs to the **Components, JSX, and Props** module.
mentalModel: Draw a decision map: **Components as building blocks** → **JSX syntax and purpose** → **React's separation-of-concerns model**. Add branches where the learner must choose an alternative and label the evidence for the choice.

sections.title: Components as building blocks
sections.body: Components divide an interface into reusable, composable units. A useful component owns one coherent responsibility and accepts explicit inputs.

sections.title: JSX syntax and purpose
sections.body: JSX is JavaScript syntax for describing element trees. Expressions appear in braces, attributes use React-oriented names, and JSX compiles to element creation calls.

sections.title: React's separation-of-concerns model
sections.body: React often colocates JSX with the state and behavior that determine it. Concerns are separated by component responsibility rather than file type alone.

sections.title: Worked example
sections.explanation: JSX describes an element tree and the function supplies a reusable boundary.

commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.
summary: Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns connects Components as building blocks, JSX syntax and purpose, React's separation-of-concerns model.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

questions.prompt: Which change is the best fix?

choices.feedback: This choice does not resolve the assessed issue. Re-trace the Components, JSX, and Separation of Concerns rule and the observable result.

choices.label: Wrap the siblings in a fragment (`<>...</>`) or an appropriate semantic container.
choices.feedback: Wrap the siblings in a fragment (`<>...</>`) or an appropriate semantic container.

questions.hint: Choose the smallest change that fixes the underlying model, not a workaround that hides the symptom.

assessmentProfile.label: Code-reading, debugging, and implementation challenge
assessmentProfile.rationale: The topic represents a day-to-day coding skill. The topic quiz must primarily test code reading, debugging, output prediction, or implementation choice.
assessmentProfile.inLessonPolicy: Use concise concept checks; coding and hybrid topics include at least one code-reading check.
assessmentProfile.topicQuizPolicy: Questions Q04–Q08 form the topic quiz. The presentation mix follows this profile.
